events.title: Birth of the Buddha
events.description: Siddhartha Gautama was born in Lumbini (present-day Nepal) as a prince of the Shakya clan.

events.title: Childhood as Siddhartha
events.description: The young prince was named Siddhartha, meaning one who has accomplished his aim, and was raised in luxury at Kapilavastu.

events.title: Great Renunciation
events.description: At age 29, Prince Siddhartha left the palace, his family, and royal life to seek liberation from suffering.

events.title: Marriage to Princess Yasodhara
events.description: At age 16, Siddhartha married Yasodhara, daughter of King Suppabuddha, and lived the life of a royal prince.

events.title: Study with Alara Kalama
events.description: Siddhartha studied meditation under the ascetic Alara Kalama and mastered the realm of nothingness.

events.title: Birth of Rahula
events.description: Siddhartha and Yasodhara had a son named Rahula, born shortly before the Great Renunciation.

events.title: Study with Uddaka Ramaputta
events.description: Siddhartha then studied under Uddaka Ramaputta, attaining the realm of neither perception nor non-perception.

events.title: The Four Great Sights
events.description: Siddhartha encountered an old man, a sick person, a corpse, and an ascetic, awakening him to the reality of suffering.

events.title: Six Years of Austerities
events.description: Siddhartha practiced extreme asceticism with five companions near Uruvela, nearly starving himself before abandoning harsh penance.

events.title: Sujata's Offering of Milk-Rice
events.description: A village woman named Sujata offered milk-rice to the ascetic, restoring his strength on the eve of enlightenment.

events.title: Enlightenment (Bodhi)
events.description: At age 35, Siddhartha attained enlightenment under the Bodhi tree at Bodh Gaya, becoming the Buddha.

events.title: Mara's Temptation
events.description: On the night of enlightenment, the Buddha overcame the temptations and fears personified as Mara, the lord of illusion.

events.title: First Sermon (Dhammacakkappavattana)
events.description: The Buddha delivered his first teaching at Deer Park in Sarnath, setting the Wheel of Dhamma in motion.

events.title: Ordination of the First Monks
events.description: The Buddha established the bhikkhu sangha when he ordained the first five disciples with the simple formula Come, monk.

events.title: Bodhi Tree Tradition at Bodh Gaya
events.description: The Bodhi tree at Bodh Gaya has been venerated since the Buddha's enlightenment, with saplings carried to Sri Lanka and beyond.

events.title: Buddha Meets King Bimbisara
events.description: King Bimbisara of Magadha became a devoted lay supporter and donated Veluvana monastery to the sangha.

events.title: First Rains Retreat (Vassa)
events.description: The Buddha and his disciples observed the first annual three-month rains retreat at Varanasi, establishing the vassa tradition.

events.title: Jetavana Monastery Gifted
events.description: The wealthy lay disciple Anathapindika purchased and donated Jetavana grove near Savatthi, a major center of teaching.

events.title: Buddha Visits Kapilavastu
events.description: The Buddha returned to his homeland and ordained his son Rahula, half-brother Nanda, and cousin Ananda.

events.title: Subduing the Mad Elephant Nalagiri
events.description: The Buddha calmed a raging elephant sent to attack him through the power of loving-kindness and composure.

events.title: Buddhist Learning at Taxila
events.description: Taxila in Gandhara was an ancient center of learning where Buddhist philosophy and medicine were studied alongside other sciences.

events.title: Devadatta's Schism Attempt
events.description: The Buddha's cousin Devadatta sought to split the sangha and proposed stricter rules, but the community remained united.

events.title: Mahaparinirvana
events.description: The Buddha passed away at Kushinagar at age 80, entering final nirvana.

events.title: First Buddhist Council
events.description: Held at Rajagaha under King Ajatasattu, presided by Mahakassapa. The Vinaya and Suttas were recited.

events.title: Buddha Relics Enshrined Across India
events.description: After the Buddha's parinirvana, his relics were distributed among eight kingdoms and enshrined in stupas across India.

events.title: Establishment of the Bhikkhuni Sangha
events.description: Mahapajapati Gotami and other women received ordination, founding the order of Buddhist nuns under the Eight Garudhammas.

events.title: Second Buddhist Council
events.description: Held at Vesali to address disputes over monastic rules.

events.title: Schism of the Mahasanghika
events.description: Disputes after the Second Council led to the first major schism, giving rise to the Mahasanghika school.

events.title: Theravada School Takes Shape
events.description: The Sthavira lineage developed into what later became known as Theravada, emphasizing the earliest recorded teachings.

events.title: Rise of the Sarvastivada School
events.description: The Sarvastivada school flourished in northwest India, producing extensive Abhidharma literature.

events.title: Ashoka's Conversion to Buddhism
events.description: After the Kalinga war, Emperor Ashoka embraced Buddhism and resolved to govern by Dhamma rather than conquest.

events.title: Mauryan Royal Patronage of Buddhism
events.description: Beyond Ashoka, the Mauryan court supported monasteries and missionary activity across the Indian subcontinent.

events.title: Ashoka's Dhamma-Vijaya Policy
events.description: Ashoka replaced military conquest with Dhamma-vijaya — victory through righteousness — as the guiding principle of his empire.

events.title: Ashoka's Dhamma Edicts
events.description: Ashoka inscribed moral and religious teachings on pillars and rocks across his empire, spreading Buddhist ethics widely.

events.title: Rock Edicts Across the Subcontinent
events.description: Ashoka's rock edicts at Dhauli, Girnar, and Kalsi spread Buddhist ethical teachings in Prakrit across diverse regions of India.

events.title: Compilation of Abhidhamma Texts
events.description: Systematic philosophical texts analyzing mind, matter, and ethics were compiled, later central to Theravada scholarship.

events.title: Ashoka Sends Buddhist Missionaries
events.description: Ashoka dispatched monks to Sri Lanka, Kashmir, Gandhara, and beyond to spread the Dhamma.

events.title: Sarnath Lion Capital
events.description: Ashoka's Lion Capital at Sarnath, with its four lions and Dhamma wheel, became India's national emblem.

events.title: Mauryan Buddhist Stupas
events.description: Ashoka and his successors expanded and built stupas at Sanchi, Bharhut, and other sites across the Mauryan empire.

events.title: Ashoka Pillars Erected Across India
events.description: Ashoka erected polished sandstone pillars with Dhamma inscriptions from Afghanistan to Karnataka across his empire.

events.title: Third Buddhist Council
events.description: Held at Pataliputra under Emperor Ashoka, led by Moggaliputta Tissa.

events.title: Buddhism Arrives in Sri Lanka
events.description: Emperor Ashoka sent his son Mahinda to Sri Lanka, where King Devanampiya Tissa received the Dhamma at Mihintale.

events.title: Bodhi Tree Sapling to Sri Lanka
events.description: Ashoka's daughter Sanghamitta brought a sapling of the original Bodhi tree to Anuradhapura, where it remains revered today.

events.title: Greco-Buddhist Art in Gandhara
events.description: Buddhist sculpture in Gandhara blended Greek artistic forms with Buddhist themes, producing the first iconic Buddha images.

events.title: Sunga Dynasty Buddhist Art
events.description: The Sunga period saw flourishing Buddhist stupa decoration at Sanchi and Bharhut, narrating Jataka tales in stone.

events.title: Rise of Mahayana Buddhism
events.description: New sutras and the bodhisattva ideal expanded Buddhism beyond monastic arhatship toward universal liberation.

events.title: Buddhism Under the Kushan Empire
events.description: Kushan rulers patronized Buddhism across Gandhara and Mathura, fostering trade-route monasteries and art.

events.title: Bharhut Stupa Reliefs
events.description: The Bharhut stupa reliefs in Madhya Pradesh preserve some of the earliest narrative depictions of the Buddha's life.

events.title: Fourth Buddhist Council (Sri Lanka)
events.description: Theravada monks in Sri Lanka committed the Pali Tipitaka to writing at Aluvihara to preserve the teachings in a time of famine.

events.title: Jataka Tales Compiled in India
events.description: Collections of Jataka tales recounting the Buddha's past lives were compiled in Pali and Sanskrit, spreading moral teachings across Asia.

events.title: Buddhism Enters China
events.description: Emperor Ming of Han welcomed Buddhist monks and scriptures from India, marking the formal beginning of Buddhism in China.

events.title: Fourth Buddhist Council (Kashmir)
events.description: Under King Kanishka, scholars of the Sarvastivada school systematized their Abhidharma texts in Kashmir.

events.title: Lotus Sutra Gains Prominence
events.description: The Saddharmapundarika Sutra became one of the most influential Mahayana scriptures across East Asia.

events.title: Buddhist Art School of Mathura
events.description: Mathura in northern India produced distinctive early Buddha images that influenced Buddhist art across Asia.

events.title: Buddhist Cave Monasteries of the Deccan
events.description: Rock-cut cave monasteries at Bhaja, Bedsa, Karla, and later Ajanta and Ellora housed thousands of monks across centuries.

events.title: Women Patrons of Indian Buddhism
events.description: Lay women such as Visakha and Queen Mallika supported the sangha, building monasteries and funding the Buddha's missions.

events.title: Nagarjuna and the Madhyamaka School
events.description: The philosopher Nagarjuna articulated the Middle Way philosophy of emptiness (shunyata), reshaping Mahayana thought.

events.title: Buddhism Introduced to Vietnam
events.description: Buddhist teachings reached Vietnam through both Chinese Mahayana and Indian maritime trade routes.

events.title: Buddhism Along the Silk Road
events.description: Monasteries and cave temples along Central Asian trade routes carried Buddhist teachings between India and China.

events.title: Kanheri Buddhist Caves
events.description: The Kanheri caves near Mumbai served as a major Buddhist monastic and educational center for over a millennium.

events.title: Amaravati Stupa
events.description: The Great Stupa at Amaravati in Andhra Pradesh was a magnificent center of Buddhist art and pilgrimage in ancient India.

events.title: Sanskrit Mahayana Sutras in India
events.description: New Mahayana sutras composed in Sanskrit in India spread to China, Tibet, and Southeast Asia, reshaping Buddhist practice.

events.title: Mogao Caves at Dunhuang
events.description: Buddhist cave temples at Dunhuang in China became a vast repository of art, manuscripts, and meditation practice.

events.title: Buddhism Introduced to Korea
events.description: Buddhist scriptures and monks reached the kingdom of Goguryeo, beginning a long and influential Korean Buddhist tradition.

events.title: Faxian's Pilgrimage to India
events.description: Chinese monk Faxian traveled to India and Sri Lanka to obtain Vinaya texts and record the state of Buddhist sites.

events.title: Vasubandhu and the Yogacara School
events.description: The philosopher Vasubandhu developed Yogacara teachings on consciousness, deeply influencing Mahayana meditation theory.

events.title: Gupta Golden Age of Buddhist Art
events.description: The Gupta period produced refined Buddha images at Sarnath and Nalanda, considered the classical ideal of Buddhist sculpture.

events.title: Kumarajiva Translates Buddhist Texts
events.description: The monk Kumarajiva translated key Mahayana sutras into Chinese, shaping East Asian Buddhism for centuries.

events.title: Pure Land School Develops in China
events.description: Devotional practices centered on Amitabha Buddha developed into the Pure Land school, later widespread across East Asia.

events.title: Buddhaghosa Composes Visuddhimagga
events.description: Scholar-monk Buddhaghosa wrote the Path of Purification in Sri Lanka, systematizing Theravada meditation and doctrine.

events.title: Nalanda Mahavihara Founded
events.description: The great monastic university at Nalanda in Bihar became a renowned center of Buddhist philosophy, logic, and meditation.

events.title: Buddhism in the Nepal Valley
events.description: Buddhist stupas and monasteries flourished in the Kathmandu Valley under Licchavi and later dynasties.

events.title: Decline of Buddhism in Northwest India
events.description: Huna invasions and loss of royal patronage weakened Buddhist monasteries in Gandhara and northwest India.

events.title: Mahavamsa Chronicle Composed
events.description: The Mahavamsa chronicle in Sri Lanka recorded Buddhist history and royal patronage, preserving Theravada heritage.

events.title: Bodhidharma Brings Chan to China
events.description: The Indian patriarch Bodhidharma is traditionally credited with transmitting Chan (Zen) meditation to the Shaolin monastery in China.

events.title: Odantapuri Mahavihara Founded
events.description: The monastic university at Odantapuri in Bihar became a major center of Buddhist learning under Pala patronage.

events.title: Buddhism Arrives in Japan
events.description: Buddhist images and texts were presented to the Japanese court from Korea, initiating Buddhism as a major force in Japan.

events.title: Tiantai School Founded
events.description: Zhiyi founded the Tiantai school in China, systematizing meditation and the Lotus Sutra as central teachings.

events.title: Wonhyo Unifies Korean Buddhist Thought
events.description: The Korean monk Wonhyo harmonized competing Buddhist schools, making the Dhamma accessible to common people.

events.title: Ellora Buddhist Caves
events.description: Buddhist cave temples at Ellora in Maharashtra were carved alongside Hindu and Jain monuments, showcasing religious coexistence.

events.title: Prince Shotoku Promotes Buddhism
events.description: Prince Shotoku Taishi of Japan promoted Buddhist governance, built temples, and sent missions to China.

events.title: Xuanzang's Journey to India
events.description: The Chinese pilgrim Xuanzang spent sixteen years in India studying at Nalanda and brought back hundreds of Sanskrit texts.

events.title: King Harsha Patronizes Buddhism
events.description: Emperor Harsha of Kannauj was a generous patron of Buddhism, hosting the Chinese pilgrim Xuanzang and supporting monasteries.

events.title: Xuanzang Records Nalanda's Glory
events.description: The Chinese pilgrim Xuanzang described Nalanda as having thousands of students and hundreds of teachers at its peak.

events.title: Buddhism Established in Tibet
events.description: King Songtsen Gampo married Buddhist princesses and promoted the Dhamma, laying foundations for Tibetan Buddhism.

events.title: Yijing's Pilgrimage to India
events.description: The Chinese monk Yijing spent twenty-five years in India and Southeast Asia, documenting monastic life and Vinaya practice.

events.title: Huayan School Founded
events.description: The Huayan (Flower Garland) school synthesized Mahayana sutras into a philosophy of interconnected reality.

events.title: Vajrayana Buddhism Emerges in India
events.description: Tantric Buddhist practices developed at Nalanda and Vikramashila, later transmitted to Tibet, Nepal, and East Asia.

events.title: Buddhism Becomes State Religion of Bhutan
events.description: Buddhist teachings took root in Bhutan under Tibetan influence, later becoming the foundation of national identity.

events.title: Pala Dynasty Patronage of Buddhism
events.description: The Pala kings of Bengal and Bihar became great patrons of Nalanda, Vikramashila, and Buddhist scholarship in India.

events.title: Buddhism in Maritime Southeast Asia
events.description: Srivijaya and other kingdoms in Sumatra and the Malay world supported Mahayana and Vajrayana learning and trade-route Buddhism.

events.title: Shantarakshita Establishes Buddhism in Tibet from India
events.description: The Indian scholar Shantarakshita traveled to Tibet at the invitation of King Trisong Detsen, founding Samye Monastery.

events.title: Samye Monastery Founded
events.description: The first Buddhist monastery in Tibet was established at Samye under King Trisong Detsen and Guru Padmasambhava.

events.title: Buddhism Under the Mongol Yuan
events.description: Tibetan Buddhism gained imperial patronage under Kublai Khan, linking Mongol rule with Tibetan lamas and monastic institutions.

events.title: Vikramashila Mahavihara Founded
events.description: The great monastic university at Vikramashila in Bihar rivaled Nalanda as a center of tantric and philosophical learning.

events.title: Ratnagiri Buddhist Monastery
events.description: The monastery at Ratnagiri in Odisha was a major tantric Buddhist center under Pala dynasty patronage.

events.title: Somapura Mahavihara
events.description: The Pala dynasty built Somapura Mahavihara in present-day Bangladesh, a vast monastic complex and UNESCO World Heritage Site.

events.title: Saicho Founds Tendai School in Japan
events.description: The monk Saicho established the Tendai school on Mount Hiei after studying Tiantai teachings in China.

events.title: Padmasambhava Establishes Vajrayana in Tibet
events.description: Guru Padmasambhava is credited with subduing obstacles and establishing tantric Buddhist practice in Tibet.

events.title: Kukai Founds Shingon Buddhism
events.description: The monk Kukai returned from China and established Shingon esoteric Buddhism, building temples across Japan.

events.title: Borobudur Temple Completed
events.description: The massive Mahayana monument in Java was completed, embodying the path to enlightenment in stone reliefs and stupas.

events.title: Buddhism in Ladakh
events.description: Tibetan Buddhism flourished in Ladakh, building monasteries such as Hemis, Thiksey, and Diskit across the Himalayan region.

events.title: Atisha Revives Buddhism in Tibet
events.description: The Indian master Atisha Dipankara spent twelve years in Tibet, reforming monastic discipline and inspiring the Kadampa tradition.

events.title: Atisha's Mission from India to Tibet
events.description: The Bengali master Atisha Dipankara, trained at Nalanda, spent his final years reviving Buddhism in Tibet.

events.title: Buddhist Temple Building in Bagan
events.description: The kingdom of Bagan in Myanmar built thousands of stupas and temples, creating one of the world's great Buddhist landscapes.

events.title: Theravada Buddhism in Myanmar
events.description: King Anawrahta unified Burma and made Theravada Buddhism the state religion after receiving scriptures from Sri Lanka.

events.title: Buddhism Under the Sena Dynasty
events.description: The Sena rulers of Bengal initially supported Buddhism before the tradition declined in eastern India.

events.title: Last Buddhist Strongholds in Bengal
events.description: Buddhist monasteries in Bengal and Bihar persisted under Pala patronage until the twelfth century invasions.

events.title: Angkor Wat as Buddhist Temple
events.description: The Khmer temple of Angkor Wat, originally dedicated to Vishnu, became a major center of Theravada Buddhist worship in Cambodia.

events.title: Eisai Introduces Rinzai Zen to Japan
events.description: The monk Eisai brought Rinzai Zen from China and founded Kenninji, establishing Zen as a major Japanese tradition.

events.title: Destruction of Nalanda
events.description: Turkic invaders under Bakhtiyar Khalji sacked Nalanda, ending centuries of monastic learning and marking a major decline in Indian Buddhism.

events.title: Destruction of Vikramashila
events.description: Vikramashila Mahavihara was destroyed by Turkic invasions, further devastating Buddhist institutions in eastern India.

events.title: Final Decline of Buddhism in Eastern India
events.description: Loss of monastic universities, shifting patronage, and invasions led to the near disappearance of Buddhism from its Indian heartland.

events.title: Jinul Reforms Korean Seon Buddhism
events.description: The monk Jinul integrated Seon meditation with doctrinal study, shaping Korean Buddhism for centuries.

events.title: Impact of Delhi Sultanate on Indian Buddhism
events.description: The establishment of the Delhi Sultanate accelerated the decline of already weakened Buddhist institutions in northern India.

events.title: Kandyan Vinaya Reform
events.description: Sri Lankan monks under Parakramabahu I held a council to restore higher ordination and unify monastic practice on the island.

events.title: Dogen Founds Soto Zen in Japan
events.description: The monk Dogen brought Soto Zen from China and emphasized zazen meditation as the essence of Buddhist practice.

events.title: Nichiren Buddhism Founded
events.description: The monk Nichiren founded a tradition centered on devotion to the Lotus Sutra and social reform in Japan.

events.title: Theravada Buddhism in Thailand
events.description: King Ramkhamhaeng of Sukhothai promoted Theravada Buddhism, strengthening its role in Thai culture and governance.

events.title: Buddhism in the Lao Kingdoms
events.description: Theravada Buddhism became the dominant religion across Lao kingdoms, shaping culture, art, and monastic life.

events.title: Theravada Buddhism in Cambodia
events.description: Theravada Buddhism replaced Mahayana as the dominant tradition in Cambodia, centered on Angkor and later Phnom Penh.

events.title: Tsongkhapa Founds Gelug School
events.description: The Tibetan master Tsongkhapa founded Ganden Monastery and the Gelug school, emphasizing rigorous study and ethics.

events.title: Buddhism Revived in Mongolia
events.description: Altan Khan of Mongolia conferred the title Dalai Lama on Sonam Gyatso, linking Mongolia and Tibet in Buddhist alliance.

events.title: Buddhism in Kalmykia
events.description: Kalmyk Mongols brought Tibetan Buddhism to the North Caucasus region of Russia.

events.title: Buddhism in Sikkim and the Himalayas
events.description: Tibetan Buddhism became the state religion of Sikkim under the Namgyal dynasty.

events.title: Buddhism Spreads to Buryatia
events.description: Tibetan Buddhism reached Buryat peoples of Siberia through Mongol and Tibetan missionary activity.

events.title: Buddhism in Arunachal Pradesh
events.description: Tibetan Buddhist monasteries such as Tawang Gompa were established across Arunachal Pradesh in the Himalayas.

events.title: Ajanta Cave Paintings Recognized
events.description: British officers rediscovered the Ajanta cave paintings, revealing masterpieces of ancient Indian Buddhist art to the world.

events.title: Rediscovery of Buddhist Sites in India
events.description: British and Indian archaeologists uncovered Sarnath, Sanchi, and other ancient Buddhist sites, sparking renewed interest in India's Buddhist heritage.

events.title: Buddhism Established in Australia
events.description: Chinese and Sri Lankan immigrants brought Buddhist practice to Australia, later joined by Tibetan and Vietnamese communities.

events.title: King Mongkut Reforms Thai Buddhism
events.description: King Mongkut of Thailand founded the Dhammayutika order, emphasizing strict Vinaya and Pali scholarship.

events.title: Fifth Buddhist Council (Myanmar)
events.description: Theravada monks in Mandalay recited and inscribed the Pali Canon on marble slabs during the Fifth Buddhist Council in Myanmar.

events.title: Buddhism Reaches the Western World
events.description: Edwin Arnold's The Light of Asia and early translations introduced Buddhist ideas to Europe and America.

events.title: Buddhism in New Zealand
events.description: Chinese gold miners and later Asian immigrants brought Buddhist traditions to New Zealand.

events.title: Restoration of Bodh Gaya Temple
events.description: Anagarika Dharmapala campaigned to restore Buddhist control of the Mahabodhi Temple at Bodh Gaya, the site of enlightenment.

events.title: Pali Text Society Founded
events.description: T. W. Rhys Davids founded the Pali Text Society in London, publishing critical editions of the Tipitaka for global scholarship.

events.title: Buddhism in Hawaii
events.description: Japanese immigrants established Jodo Shinshu and other Buddhist temples across the Hawaiian islands.

events.title: Maha Bodhi Society Founded
events.description: Anagarika Dharmapala founded the Maha Bodhi Society to restore Bodh Gaya and revive Buddhism in India and worldwide.

events.title: Buddhism in South Africa
events.description: Buddhist communities formed in South Africa through Asian immigration and later through Western convert traditions.

events.title: Buddhism in Singapore
events.description: Chinese, Thai, and Sri Lankan Buddhist communities built temples and schools, making Singapore a diverse Buddhist hub.

events.title: Buddhism in Malaysia
events.description: Chinese and Thai Buddhist traditions flourished in Malaysia alongside Theravada communities of Malaysian Siamese people.

events.title: Buddhism in Canada
events.description: Chinese, Japanese, and later Vietnamese and Tibetan Buddhist communities established temples across Canada.

events.title: Buddhism in Germany
events.description: German scholars and practitioners founded Buddhist societies, translating sutras and establishing meditation groups.

events.title: Restoration of Sarnath
events.description: The Archaeological Survey of India and Maha Bodhi Society restored Sarnath, where the Buddha gave his first sermon.

events.title: Modern Buddhist Revival
events.description: Reform and revival movements across Asia emphasized meditation, social ethics, and renewed study of original scriptures.

events.title: Sanchi Stupa Restoration
events.description: Sir John Marshall led restoration of the Great Stupa at Sanchi, one of India's oldest and finest Buddhist monuments.

events.title: Nalanda Archaeological Excavation
events.description: Systematic excavation of Nalanda revealed the layout of the ancient university, inspiring modern revival efforts.

events.title: Buddhism in the United Kingdom
events.description: The London Buddhist Society and Theravada missions introduced organized Buddhist practice to Britain.

events.title: Buddhism in France
events.description: French colonial contact with Vietnam and Cambodia, and later Tibetan refugees, brought Buddhist practice to France.

events.title: First Zen Center in America
events.description: Zen Buddhist practice groups formed in the United States, beginning the spread of meditation traditions in the West.

events.title: Buddhism in the Philippines
events.description: Chinese and later Tibetan and Zen Buddhist communities established practice centers across the Philippines.

events.title: Dr. Ambedkar Discovers Buddhism
events.description: Dr. B. R. Ambedkar, architect of India's Constitution, turned to Buddhism as a path of liberty, equality, and fraternity beyond caste oppression.

events.title: International Buddhist Flag Adopted
events.description: The World Fellowship of Buddhists adopted the six-colored Buddhist flag as a symbol of unity among traditions worldwide.

events.title: Buddhism in Brazil and Latin America
events.description: Japanese, Chinese, and Tibetan Buddhist traditions took root across Brazil and Latin America in the twentieth century.

events.title: Buddhism Flourishes in Taiwan
events.description: Humanistic Buddhism movements in Taiwan promoted education, charity, and meditation, making Taiwan a major center of Chinese Buddhism.

events.title: Buddhism in Scandinavia
events.description: Tibetan refugees and Western practitioners established Buddhist centers across Denmark, Norway, and Sweden.

events.title: Buddhist Values in the Indian Constitution
events.description: Dr. B. R. Ambedkar embedded principles of liberty, equality, and fraternity — drawn from his Buddhist vision — into India's Constitution.

events.title: Buddha Purnima as National Holiday in India
events.description: India declared Buddha Purnima a national holiday, honoring the Buddha's birth, enlightenment, and parinirvana.

events.title: Sixth Buddhist Council
events.description: Theravada monks from many countries gathered in Yangon, Myanmar, to recite and authenticate the Pali Tipitaka.

events.title: Ambedkar Announces Conversion to Buddhism
events.description: Dr. B. R. Ambedkar publicly declared his intention to convert to Buddhism, rejecting caste inequality and embracing the Buddha's path.

events.title: Beat Generation Embraces Buddhism
events.description: Writers such as Jack Kerouac and Allen Ginsberg popularized Buddhist ideas in American literature and counterculture.

events.title: Ambedkar's Mass Conversion at Deekshabhoomi
events.description: Dr. B. R. Ambedkar and hundreds of thousands of followers embraced Buddhism at Nagpur on Vijayadashami, affirming liberty, equality, and fraternity.

events.title: Neo-Buddhist Movement in India
events.description: Following Ambedkar's conversion, millions of Dalits across Maharashtra and India embraced Buddhism, creating a vibrant new Buddhist community.

events.title: Chaitya Bhoomi Memorial
events.description: Chaitya Bhoomi in Mumbai became the memorial and pilgrimage site where Dr. B. R. Ambedkar was cremated after his Buddhist conversion.

events.title: Ambedkar Publishes The Buddha and His Dhamma
events.description: Dr. B. R. Ambedkar wrote The Buddha and His Dhamma, presenting the Buddha's life and teachings as a rational, egalitarian philosophy for modern India.

events.title: Ambedkar Jayanti as Public Observance
events.description: April 14, Dr. Ambedkar's birth anniversary, became widely observed by Indian Buddhists as a day of reflection and community gathering.

events.title: Dalai Lama in Exile
events.description: The 14th Dalai Lama fled Tibet and established a government-in-exile in India, preserving Tibetan Buddhist culture and teachings.

events.title: Dharamsala as Tibetan Buddhist Center
events.description: The Tibetan government-in-exile established its seat in Dharamsala, India, making it a global center of Tibetan Buddhist study.

events.title: Waghala Buddhist Vihara Movement
events.description: New Buddhist viharas and schools were built across Maharashtra and India, nurturing Ambedkarite Buddhist community life.

events.title: Tibetan Buddhist Settlements in India
events.description: India welcomed Tibetan refugees, establishing settlements at Bylakuppe, Mundgod, and Dharamsala with monasteries and schools.

events.title: Buddhist Population Growth in Maharashtra
events.description: Census records showed a significant rise in Buddhist population in Maharashtra following Ambedkar's mass conversion movement.

events.title: Thich Nhat Hanh and Engaged Buddhism
events.description: Vietnamese monk Thich Nhat Hanh coined Engaged Buddhism, linking mindfulness practice with peace activism and social justice.

events.title: Fo Guang Shan Humanistic Buddhism
events.description: Master Hsing Yun founded Fo Guang Shan in Taiwan, spreading Humanistic Buddhism emphasizing education and social engagement worldwide.

events.title: Buddhist Revival in Indonesia
events.description: After centuries of decline, Buddhist communities in Indonesia experienced revival through study groups and temple restoration.

events.title: Indian Buddhist Diaspora Worldwide
events.description: Indian Buddhists, especially from the Ambedkarite community, established temples and cultural centers across the world.

events.title: Vipassana Meditation Spreads Worldwide
events.description: S. N. Goenka began teaching Vipassana meditation internationally, drawing on the Burmese tradition of Sayagyi U Ba Khin.

events.title: Sikkim Joins India as Buddhist State
events.description: Sikkim became part of India while preserving its rich Tibetan Buddhist monastic heritage and pilgrimage sites.

events.title: Dhammagiri Vipassana Center
events.description: S. N. Goenka established the Dhammagiri Vipassana center at Igatpuri, Maharashtra, one of the world's largest meditation centers.

events.title: Triratna Buddhist Community in India
events.description: The Triratna Buddhist Order expanded in India, offering Dhamma teaching and community development inspired by Ambedkar's vision.

events.title: Mindfulness-Based Stress Reduction
events.description: Dr. Jon Kabat-Zinn developed MBSR at the University of Massachusetts, bringing Buddhist mindfulness into modern healthcare.

events.title: Thai Forest Tradition Spreads Globally
events.description: Ajahn Chah and disciples established forest monasteries worldwide, teaching austere meditation practice rooted in the Thai forest tradition.

events.title: Buddhist Revival in Post-Mao China
events.description: Temples reopened and Buddhist practice revived across China after the Cultural Revolution period of suppression.

events.title: Plum Village Monastery Founded
events.description: Thich Nhat Hanh founded Plum Village in France, becoming Europe's largest Buddhist monastery and center of Engaged Buddhism.

events.title: Buddhism and Science Dialogue
events.description: The Mind and Life Institute began dialogues between the Dalai Lama and Western scientists on consciousness and well-being.

events.title: Dalai Lama Awarded Nobel Peace Prize
events.description: The 14th Dalai Lama received the Nobel Peace Prize for his nonviolent struggle for the liberation of Tibet and promotion of compassion.

events.title: Buddhist Environmental Movement
events.description: Buddhist leaders worldwide began advocating for ecological protection rooted in interdependence and compassion for all life.

events.title: Global Movement for Bhikkhuni Ordination
events.description: Theravada communities in Sri Lanka and beyond revived full ordination for women, continuing the legacy of Mahapajapati Gotami.

events.title: UN Recognition of Vesak
events.description: The United Nations recognized Vesak as an international day of observance, honoring the birth, enlightenment, and passing of the Buddha.

events.title: Buddhist Teachings Go Online
events.description: Monasteries, teachers, and scholars worldwide began sharing Dhamma talks, sutras, and meditation guidance through the internet.

events.title: Destruction of Bamiyan Buddhas
events.description: The Taliban destroyed the giant Buddha statues at Bamiyan, Afghanistan, shocking the world and highlighting the fragility of Buddhist heritage.

events.title: Mass Buddhist Initiation Ceremonies in India
events.description: Large-scale diksha ceremonies continued across India as millions embraced Buddhism inspired by Dr. Ambedkar's legacy.

events.title: Lumbini UNESCO World Heritage
events.description: The birthplace of the Buddha at Lumbini was inscribed as a UNESCO World Heritage Site, protecting a key pilgrimage destination.

events.title: Buddhist Peace Efforts in Sri Lanka
events.description: Monks and lay Buddhists in Sri Lanka worked for peace and reconciliation during and after the civil war.

events.title: Mahabodhi Temple UNESCO Heritage
events.description: The Mahabodhi Temple at Bodh Gaya was inscribed as a UNESCO World Heritage Site, recognizing its global spiritual significance.

events.title: International Buddhist Conclave at Bodh Gaya
events.description: India hosted international Buddhist conferences at Bodh Gaya, reaffirming the site's role as a global pilgrimage center.

events.title: Saffron Revolution in Myanmar
events.description: Buddhist monks led mass protests in Myanmar calling for democracy and human rights, demonstrating Engaged Buddhism in action.

events.title: Nagaloka Buddhist Training Center
events.description: The Nagaloka center in Nagpur was established to train a new generation of Indian Buddhists in meditation, study, and social transformation.

events.title: Nalanda University Revived
events.description: India re-established Nalanda University in Bihar as an international institution, symbolizing the revival of India's Buddhist scholarly heritage.

events.title: Kushinagar Buddhist Circuit Development
events.description: India developed Kushinagar, where the Buddha attained parinirvana, as a major pilgrimage and heritage destination.

events.title: Buddhist Circuit Pilgrimage in Modern India
events.description: India launched the Buddhist Circuit initiative connecting Lumbini, Bodh Gaya, Sarnath, and Kushinagar for international pilgrims.

events.title: Buddhist Communities Adapt During COVID-19
events.description: Temples and meditation centers worldwide shifted to online teachings and virtual sangha gatherings during the global pandemic.

events.title: Global Buddhist Summit
events.description: Leaders and scholars from Buddhist traditions worldwide met in New Delhi to discuss peace, compassion, and shared responsibility.
